claims.claim_text: The opening anecdote headline presents an unsourced $847,000 incident as a real event.
claims.anchor_text: **The $847,000 Friday Afternoon Deploy**
claims.suggested_rewrite: **A Costly Friday Afternoon Deploy**
claims.rationale: This is a fake-precise anecdotal headline with no attributable source.

claims.claim_text: A Series C startup's AWS bill supposedly jumped from $120,000 to $967,000 in one month.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, first story paragraph
claims.anchor_text: The infrastructure team at a Series C startup gathered for their weekly standup. Everything seemed normal until the CFO walked in, printout in hand, face ashen. "Can someone explain why our AWS bill jumped from $120,000 to $967,000 this month?"
claims.suggested_rewrite: An infrastructure team can discover a major cloud-billing spike only after finance reviews the monthly bill.
claims.rationale: This is an unattributed incident narrative with named business context and exact dollar amounts.

claims.claim_text: The story claims a Terraform config used r5.24xlarge instances at $4.80/hour, with count=10 left in a 2,000-line diff.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, second story paragraph
claims.anchor_text: The investigation traced the spike to a Friday afternoon deployment three weeks earlier. A developer had been testing a new data processing pipeline and created a Terraform configuration with `instance_type = "r5.24xlarge"` (96 vCPUs, 768GB RAM, $4.80/hour). They'd intended to use a single instance for testing, but a `count = 10` from a copy-paste remained in the code. The PR was approved—nobody noticed the instance type buried in a 2,000-line diff.
claims.suggested_rewrite: One common failure mode is leaving an expensive test configuration in a large Terraform diff, where reviewers miss a costly instance type or replica count before merge.
claims.rationale: The paragraph mixes unstable price data with an uncited, company-specific deployment story.

claims.claim_text: The story claims ten large instances ran for 21 days and caused $847,000 in unexpected spend, shortening runway by two months.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, third story paragraph
claims.anchor_text: For 21 days, ten massive instances ran 24/7, processing approximately nothing. Total unexpected cost: $847,000. The startup's runway shortened by 2 months.
claims.suggested_rewrite: If oversized resources run unnoticed for weeks, the resulting waste can materially affect a startup's budget and planning.
claims.rationale: This is an uncited war story with precise duration, cost, and business impact.

claims.claim_text: Infracost provides cost estimates for Terraform before deployment or apply.
claims.span_hint: section: Infracost: Cost Estimation in CI/CD, opening sentence
claims.rationale: The Infracost README explicitly says it shows cloud cost estimates for Terraform before making changes.

claims.claim_text: Infracost has CI/CD integrations and can post cost estimates in pull requests.
claims.span_hint: section: Infracost: Cost Estimation in CI/CD, GitHub Actions Integration / PR comment framing
claims.rationale: The project README explicitly states that Infracost has CI/CD integrations and can post estimates in pull requests.

claims.claim_text: Using only on-demand pricing misses 30-70% savings.
claims.span_hint: section: Common Mistakes, table row for always on-demand
claims.anchor_text: | Always on-demand | Missing 30-70% savings | Evaluate reserved/spot options |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Always on-demand | Missing substantial savings opportunities from commitment or spare-capacity pricing | Evaluate reserved/spot options |
claims.rationale: The exact savings range varies by service and purchase option, but the general lesson is well supported.

claims.claim_text: A $5K/day budget-alert threshold is presented as a concrete best practice.
claims.span_hint: section: War Story, Aftermath - Controls Implemented
claims.anchor_text: 3. **Daily budget alerts** - $5K/day threshold triggers investigation
claims.suggested_rewrite: 3. **Daily budget alerts** - A daily spend threshold aligned to your budget should trigger investigation
claims.rationale: The numeric threshold is illustrative rather than universally citable, but the budget-alert principle is supported.

claims.claim_text: Reserved-capacity planning supposedly offers 30-70% savings with commitment.
claims.anchor_text: - [ ] **Reserved capacity planning** - 30-70% savings available with commitment
claims.suggested_rewrite: - [ ] **Reserved capacity planning** - Commitment pricing can significantly reduce steady-state compute costs
claims.rationale: The exact savings range is too specific for a single stable source, but the underlying lesson is supported.

claims.claim_text: Flexera's 2023 State of the Cloud supposedly found average waste of 32% of cloud spend.
claims.anchor_text: > **Cost Waste Statistics**: According to Flexera's 2023 State of the Cloud report, organizations waste an average of 32% of their cloud spend on idle or underutilized resources.
claims.suggested_rewrite: > **Cost Waste Statistics**: Industry reports consistently find that idle or underutilized resources account for a meaningful share of cloud waste.
claims.rationale: The exact percentage and attribution do not line up cleanly with an allowlisted primary source.

claims.claim_text: Infracost was founded in 2020 by former Atlassian engineers.
claims.anchor_text: > **Infracost Origins**: Infracost was founded in 2020 by former Atlassian engineers who were frustrated by surprise cloud bills and wanted to bring cost visibility to the development process.
claims.suggested_rewrite: > **Infracost Origins**: Infracost emerged to bring cloud cost visibility earlier into infrastructure workflows.
claims.rationale: The exact founding narrative and founder background are not backed here by an allowlisted primary source.

claims.claim_text: A 2023 AWS study supposedly found comprehensive tagging reduced cloud costs by 18%.
claims.anchor_text: > **Tagging Impact**: A 2023 AWS study found that organizations with comprehensive tagging strategies reduced their cloud costs by an average of 18% simply through better visibility and accountability.
claims.suggested_rewrite: > **Tagging Impact**: Comprehensive tagging improves cost visibility, allocation, and accountability across teams.
claims.rationale: I did not find an allowlisted AWS primary source supporting this exact study and percentage.

claims.claim_text: Shift-left FinOps supposedly catches 85% of cost issues before production versus 23% for monthly bill review.
claims.anchor_text: > **Shift-Left Savings**: Organizations that implement cost estimation in CI/CD (shift-left FinOps) report catching 85% of cost issues before they reach production, compared to 23% with traditional monthly bill review.
claims.suggested_rewrite: > **Shift-Left Savings**: Cost estimation in CI/CD helps teams catch expensive changes earlier than end-of-month bill review.
claims.rationale: The exact comparison percentages are not supported by an authoritative allowlisted source.

claims.claim_text: The later war-story section headline presents an unsourced $847,000 copy-paste incident as real.
claims.anchor_text: ## War Story: The $847,000 Copy-Paste
claims.suggested_rewrite: ## Example Scenario: Catching an Expensive Copy-Paste
claims.rationale: This is another uncited anecdotal headline with false precision.

claims.claim_text: The module identifies the anecdote as a real Series C logistics startup.
claims.anchor_text: **Company**: Series C startup (logistics platform)
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Scenario**: A growing team is managing Terraform for a data-processing change.
claims.rationale: The company identity is presented as factual but is not sourced.

claims.claim_text: The module presents a real incident of unnoticed expensive instances running for three weeks.
claims.anchor_text: **Incident**: Unnoticed expensive instances running for 3 weeks
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Risk**: Expensive test instances run longer than intended because nobody notices the cost impact quickly.
claims.rationale: This is an unsourced incident statement with a precise duration.

claims.claim_text: The timeline says a developer opened the PR at Friday 4:30 PM.
claims.anchor_text: - **Friday 4:30 PM**: Developer creates PR for data pipeline
claims.suggested_rewrite: - A developer opens a PR for a data pipeline change.
claims.rationale: This is a fake-precise event timestamp with no source.

claims.claim_text: The timeline says a reviewer approved at Friday 4:45 PM and missed the instance type in a 2,000-line diff.
claims.anchor_text: - **Friday 4:45 PM**: Reviewer approves (2,000 line diff, missed instance type)
claims.suggested_rewrite: - A reviewer approves a large diff without noticing an expensive instance type.
claims.rationale: This is another uncited timeline event with false-specific detail.

claims.claim_text: The timeline says the change was merged and deployed at Friday 5:00 PM.
claims.anchor_text: - **Friday 5:00 PM**: Merged and deployed
claims.suggested_rewrite: - The change is merged and deployed.
claims.rationale: The specific timestamp is part of an unsourced incident chronology.

claims.claim_text: The timeline says ten r5.24xlarge instances launched at Friday 5:15 PM and cost $48/hour total.
claims.anchor_text: - **Friday 5:15 PM**: 10x r5.24xlarge instances launch ($48/hour total)
claims.suggested_rewrite: - Multiple large instances launch, creating a steep hourly cost.
claims.rationale: This combines fake timeline precision with unstable pricing and incident specifics.

claims.claim_text: The timeline says the instances ran idle and unnoticed for days 1-21.
claims.anchor_text: - **Days 1-21**: Instances run idle, nobody notices
claims.suggested_rewrite: - The instances run mostly idle for an extended period without attracting attention.
claims.rationale: This is an uncited incident-duration claim.

claims.claim_text: The timeline says the bill arrived on day 22 at $967,000 versus a normal $120,000.
claims.anchor_text: - **Day 22**: Monthly bill arrives: $967,000 (vs $120,000 normal)
claims.suggested_rewrite: - Finance eventually notices an unusually large monthly bill.
claims.rationale: This is a false-precision billing claim within an unsourced incident.

claims.claim_text: The timeline says there was a frantic investigation on days 22-23.
claims.anchor_text: - **Day 22-23**: Frantic investigation
claims.suggested_rewrite: - The team scrambles to investigate the spike.
claims.rationale: This is an unsourced incident-timeline detail.

claims.claim_text: The timeline says the root cause was found and the instances were terminated on day 23.
claims.anchor_text: - **Day 23**: Root cause found, instances terminated
claims.suggested_rewrite: - The team identifies the root cause and terminates the resources.
claims.rationale: This is another uncited event in a fabricated-looking timeline.

claims.claim_text: The timeline states the total unexpected cost was $847,000.
claims.anchor_text: - **Total unexpected cost**: $847,000
claims.suggested_rewrite: - **Total unexpected cost**: A large unplanned spend increase.
claims.rationale: This precise total is unsourced and should not remain as a factual claim.

further_reading.title: Infracost README
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary product documentation for Terraform cost estimation and pull-request integrations.

further_reading.title: Organizing and Tracking Costs Using AWS Cost Allocation Tags
further_reading.why_relevant: Authoritative AWS guidance on tagging, activation, and cost-allocation behavior.

further_reading.title: Managing Your Costs with AWS Budgets
further_reading.why_relevant: Covers actual versus forecasted alerts and budget actions used in cost-governance workflows.

notes: Per instructions, I did not seed Mermaid diagrams, code blocks, quiz items, quiz answers, or hands-on exercise steps. I seeded prose, list items, and the single claim-bearing table row that used a specific savings range.
